statement: The EU Posted Workers Directive (96/71/EC, revised 2018/957) has been incorporated into Icelandic law through the EEA Agreement. The directive requires that workers temporarily posted to Iceland receive the same minimum terms and conditions as local workers, including minimum pay, working hours, and health and safety standards. In practice, posted workers in Iceland are relatively few — estimated at 2,000–3,000 per year, primarily in construction and specialised industrial projects (aluminium smelter maintenance, geothermal drilling). Full EU membership would bring Iceland under the European Labour Authority's (ELA) oversight for cross-border enforcement, potentially improving compliance monitoring.
source_name: European Commission — Posted Workers Directive; Icelandic Ministry of Social Affairs — transposition records
caveats: Iceland's geographic isolation limits the practical relevance of posted workers compared to continental EU countries with land borders. The revised directive's 'equal pay' provisions have been controversial in some EU states but align well with Iceland's already high-wage environment.

related_entries.statement: Iceland's labour market regulation differs from the EU norm in several key respects. Union density is approximately 90% (vs EU average ~23%), and wages are set primarily through centralised collective agreements between ASÍ and SA rather than through legislation or sectoral bargaining. There is no statutory minimum wage — the collective agreement minimum functions as the de facto floor. Iceland has already transposed most EU employment directives through the EEA, including working time, equal treatment, and posted workers rules. Key areas where EU membership could bring additional regulation include works councils (European Works Council Directive has limited EEA application), co-determination, and the proposed EU minimum wage directive.

Iceland already participates in the free movement of workers through the EEA Agreement, which has been in force since 1994. EEA nationals have the right to live and work in Iceland without work permits, and Icelanders have reciprocal rights across the EU/EEA. EU membership would not create new free movement rights beyond what already exists under the EEA framework. However, EU membership would give Iceland voting rights in shaping the rules governing free movement, whereas under the EEA Iceland implements EU single market legislation without a formal vote in the Council or European Parliament.
